sentences.text: My name is Betty Cohen, C-O-H-E-N.
I was born Betty Straus, S-T-R-A-U-S.
I was born in S'Heerenberg, Holland, 1920.
It's S apostrophe-H-E-E-R-E-N-B-E-R-G, which translated means the-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
The garden, garden of the Lord.
The mountain of the Lord, actually.
But I imagine that you're quite used to them.
Oh, yes.
I am.
The only thing I know is my mother's mother, she was-- she lived in Germany.
And my mother and I visited her once.
She was, in my opinion-- I was about five-- she was a very old lady.
Maybe-- you know, so maybe she was not that old, but in my opinion, she was very, very old.
And I saw her only once.
It was a train trip that we went-- when we went over there.
It was a train trip, lasted about six hours.
So it was far, you know, when you went that far.
That's the only one.
The rest, I have no idea where they were, or-- I-- but in my lifetime they were not around anymore.
From what I know, from my father's family and-- they were born-- my grandparents were already born in Holland, my father's parents.
So I think that-- and that's probably why we have never been in ghettos or anything, because in Holland, we didn't have ghettos.
My mother, however, who was German, who was born in Germany, she came what's-- from the Sephardic side.
So I do not know how and where, but I only know that that's what the story was.
I was the youngest.
I was the youngest of four children, terribly spoiled by my father.
Also spoiled by my Aunt Betty.
I had-- since I was the youngest and everybody was already in school, my aunt who lived in Germany in the city of Essen, would come on a regular basis, once a month at least.
And she would come on Friday evening or Friday afternoon and stay over Shabbat and go back and take me back on Sunday morning.
And we would travel to Essen, which was 90 kilometers, 60 miles approximately, 60 miles.

And since I was born in a very, very, very small town, she was the one who taught me manners and who also, as a very small child, would take me out to restaurants where, in German, which was not my regular language, I had to read the menus.
And I had to order whatever I needed to eat.
She thought it was terribly important for me to know all these things.
So all these little social rules and regulations I learned from my Aunt Betty, who would take me anyplace.
Plus, I was all of five years old, and my only-- my Aunt Betty's only son was already studying to be an attorney.
So all his friends were about 19, 20, 19, 20 years old.
And they used me as their-- well, maybe I should say mascot.
That would be a very good word.
And they also would call me and make dates with me.
So I learned how to-- I had to turn around to my aunt, whether I could go with these gentleman.
They always had music halls to go to or plays for children to go to.
So I was terribly spoiled during that time.
That was a beautiful time for me.
My aunt, who was-- I think she was a hat maker, ladies' hat maker originally.
She was very much in beautiful clothing and everything.
She loved that.
And I would go along to shops, to stores and get-- everything had to be matching, a coat that was made for me.
And then we would go to a store and buy a-- it was friends of my aunts.
And I would not have to buy it.
I would get as a present a matching purse or anything like that.
So if you say I was spoiled too often during that time, you're probably right.
I was.
I was terribly spoiled.
Tell me about your brothers and sisters.
OK.
OK.
There were two brothers, one two years older than I am.
And he was the closest to me.
We--
It was Harry.
And he was-- well, we-- he wouldn't play soccer unless his sister could play soccer also.
So I was always in the soccer team.
We would play soccer on the-- on the street, actually.
I told you we lived in a very, very small town.
Real, very old-fashioned little town.
The town also had a castle, a real castle, 1300-and-something, with a wall around the castle, which was actually our back wall.
If I stepped out of the house, there was the wall that was surrounding the little city.
And if I nowadays say that I learned to ice skate on the, the water that was around the castle, everybody looks at-- huh?
Ice skating on castle-- around a castle?
But that's where I learned.
I was about four or five years old, I would say.
My father was very much always sporty and so on.
So were my brothers, so I learned how to skate when I was about five years.
Ice skate, I'm talking about.
My brother was there too.
And we would always go together with a lot of special things.
Then the next-- the next brother, Joseph, he was called 'little brother.' Everybody called him [NON-ENGLISH], which is 'little brother.' A genius, unbelievable smart, top of the class.
He now speaks seven languages, but at the time, he only spoke three or four.
He was unbelievable smart.
Now he would probably go to, you know, whatever schools were the best for him.
But we didn't have too much of a choice where to go to school.
But he did get a very good education.
I'll just gave you an example.
When he finally-- before he did his final in this particular school, there were eight possibilities that he did not have to take an oral exam, that he could only do the written exam.
And he, he didn't have to do seven written-- oral exams, because he did so well in everything else.
And he was always there.
And the only thing that it did to me, he was-- it-- it hindered me because I tried so hard to catch up with him.
But it never worked.
He was always the smart one.
And he-- if my mother would say, why don't you help Betty with her algebra, because it was not my favorite, then he would say, Mama, she is so dumb.
And maybe teachers at the time were not maybe psychologists or anything like that, But they would always say to me, or very often say to me after a test, they would say, very good test, Betty.
Very, very good test.
Not as good as your brother, but a very, very good test.
And I know that it stayed with me all my life.
I've always tried to catch up with my brother, which I have never been able to do.
So I just decided, well, this was it.
Then my-- the old-- And the other sister?
Then the oldest one was my sister, who when she was young-- I'm not sure exactly how old, I think six or seven years old-- had the measles and ended up with very poor eyesight.
I think people didn't know exactly what happened.
But I only know my sister with very, very poor eyesight.
She was also out of the house for years, because she went to Germany to be operated on.
They were the better doctors and we had better connections there.
And she would live with my aunt.
In fact, she was gone for so long that when she came back when she was about 12 or 13, my mother had to hire a teacher who would teach her the Holland language again.
She only spoke German when she came back, because she had been gone for so long.
Yes.
Very close to the German border.
Oh, we-- there was a very small-- well, you couldn't even call it a river.
But let's say there was a very small brook.
And I would ask my company, would you like to go and stand in Holland with one leg and stand with one leg in Germany?
Now, everybody always wanted that, especially when you're six, seven years old.
So we would walk over to the border.
And it was very simple to do.
The bridge actually was half-Dutch and half-German.
So it was very simple.
We would take-- sometimes we would take chalk and we would go and make a line in the middle of the bridge and then stand indeed with one leg in Holland and one leg in Germany.
And it was fun.
We thought it was fun at least.
Mhm.
--excuse me.
A very, very devoted gymnast.
Very devoted.
Very devoted.
In this very small town, that was actually-- I think it was, I was about four years old.
So I was never a part of it.
I only of it, and I've seen things about it.
The very first I think remembrance of my father that I have was where my father was making a handstand on a chair.
That is as far as I go back.
That is the thing that I remember very, very well.
My father was a very civic-minded one.
In the little town where I lived, there were only 40, 4-0, Jews.
So that included grandfathers and grandchildren.
My father was the Jewish community.
I cannot say anything else.
He was a rabbi.
He-- he made sure there was-- as I said, there were so few Jews.
But my brother sometimes was sent out to another family to make sure that the boys there would come to the synagogue on Shabbat, because we would not have minyan otherwise.
Everybody had to come every Saturday.
And otherwise, we would not have minyan.
And we usually had minyan every Saturday.
And my parents were religious, but the religion was something that was actually at home only.
It was not outside of the house, because everybody else in this little town was Catholic.
I would say probably 80% Catholic, maybe 10% Protestant.
So all my friends and everybody I knew was Catholic.
And it didn't matter what you were, because your religion was something that you lived at home.
And the rest, you know, came-- was immaterial.
I had piano lessons for seven years.
And my piano teachers were nuns.
I would go on Tuesday morning at eight o'clock in the morning and have my piano lessons.
And I would go on Thursday morning.
And they were nuns.
And they taught me real-- what should I say?
Well, maybe I was not supposed to play without music.
It had to be-- I had to read it from the pages.
And I had to hold my hands in a certain position with a candy on the top.
And if I lifted my fingers properly, and the candy did not fall off, I could eat the candy.
If not, then they would take the candy away.
OK?
So it was a very, very, very small town.
I do not really know how many people.
There were maybe three or four streets.
I'll give you an idea.
My-- our telephone number was number four.
So there were not too many people with telephones.
And if there was anything that had anything to do with civic doings, exhibits, or whatever it was, my father was the one who commanded it, who helped everybody to get to the proper places.
He did an enormous amount of very, very good things.
And he also helped us a great deal with the-- the, well, religious parts.
Friday night was, of course, Shabbat.
And we would sit and we would ask, Papa, will you sing for us?
And Papa would sing Hebrew songs.
And it was just-- you know, it was-- the religion was something that you had at home and you didn't drag it out in the street or anything like that.
Oh, yes, definitely so.

My father was a shochet, so he would go in the surrounding little towns and do his work there, and also go across the border to little German towns, Emmerich and Elten, which were the towns very close to us.

We didn't have the idea that it was actually a border holding one person away from somebody else, because although we had passports and we used passports all along-- I was something like, let's say, five years old when I got my first passport.
So that was-- you know, by 10 years old, you had your second one.
But you had them.
But if you didn't have it, there were enough people who would know, well, you know, that you lived there.
So there was no problem.
And--
My mother was very exact, as German as they come.
[LAUGHS]
She never learned to speak Dutch very well.
So she would say one sentence and, and then she would use two languages.
But we were so used to this, that we didn't even know the difference.
She would start a sentence with a Dutch word, which is, [DUTCH], listen.
OK?
And the rest of the sentence was German.
But nobody paid any attention.
We would just answer her.
We would answer my father in Dutch if he spoke to us.
If my mother spoke to us, we would answer in German.
Who-- you know, there was no problem.
I learned-- I mean, it was terribly important, apparently, to have good manners.
I remember that I was supposed to sit on little chairs and make sure that my pleated skirt was all over the place and not over-- you know, above my knees, and all kinds of little things.
And you make, make a [GERMAN], I think that's what the Germans call it.
And you would kind of kneel down a tiny little bit if your mother would introduce you to somebody.
Good morning, Mrs.
Such-and-So, and that kind of thing.
It was terribly important.
Good manners were terribly important.
It helped me a great deal later in my life, because I was born in such a small town.
And when you later on go over the whole world, if you have good manners or if you have at least some manners, you get along anyplace.
It doesn't really matter.

One of the funny things I wanted to tell you, my job during Yom Tov was the day before-- because I'm thinking of it now, because next week is Yom Kippur, the-- my job before Yom Kippur was I had to tear the toilet paper, because we could not tear toilet paper on Yom Kippur.
That's unheard of, right?
And then-- so those were the beautiful little things.
And then Pesach Seder, my father, of course, who spoke-- who could read, you know, Hebrew very, very well-- and we, of course, learned it also.
And we went to Hebrew school on Sundays and so on.
But he could pronounce it and read it faster than we did.
One of the games for Seder was who could sing [HEBREW] the quickest.
And my father always won.
So that-- those were the things that we did at home.
My mother was very precise, very exact.
Her standard saying was [SPEAKING GERMAN].
In other words, there's got to be order in this place.
And I think, after all these years, I still live by that.
Everything has to be in its place so that you can find everything within two seconds.
And that was-- Then she was an excellent seamstress.
She had worked in Frankfurt an Mein before she was married to my father and had done an awful lot of-- made dresses and costumes for people in the theater.
And she was-- so she knew a lot about theater.
And we were very musically-- well, there was no entertainment in this little town unless we did it ourselves.
So of the four of us, the three of us, except my younger brother Harry, we all play the piano.
And my older brother taught me the original notes.
And then later on, I went to piano lessons, where the nuns taught me to play the piano better than what I knew in the beginning.
The original piano lesson was on a little play piano that had eight notes on it.
So that's my first experience there.
And it was just-- Then I wanted to let you know how we lived at home.
In this small town where there was absolutely no entertainment, we had-- very hard to believe nowadays-- two newspapers, a morning newspapers and an evening newspaper.
And my father-- we would sit around a table at night, all of us, either making homework or my mother darning socks.
That time, we didn't have nylon socks, so the socks had to be darned.
And my father then would read the newspaper aloud to all of us, got me interested in politics when I was about five, I suppose.
And would read my mother her fashion shows that she would know what was going on in the world.
But mainly it was done-- my sister had such poor eyesight that she couldn't read or read very little, and she had to know what was going on in the world also.
So that's how we were brought up.
The story, the, the home life was unbelievable beautiful.
We also had-- my parents were born in 1882, '81 and '82.
We had boxes full of books, because my father was a member of a book club.
I don't think there are that many people who have-- at that time who were members of book clubs.

But you know, so if I see now articles about people-- like last week in the they had articles about people who were almost close to 100 years old, they did exactly the same thing that I did, only they-- I mean, I did it at a younger age.
It was a beautiful life.
I think I went to school till I was about 15 or 16.
And then for-- I think I was 16.
But I have to tell you one thing, that during that time, it's coming closer to the war.
I had so much-- so many people in Germany that were related to me, that the war didn't come to me in 1940 like it did the rest of Holland.
To me personally, in our household, the war came to us in 1934, I would say, 1933, when I was still going to Germany for the weekends and so on.
I remember distinctly in 1933 when I was-- went to Essen, I saw a gentleman, Jewish gentleman, with two little girls who were about my age.
They were twins.
And he was walking on the street in Germany, but he was completely bowled over forward, like he didn't want anybody to see anything.
And then one thing I remember distinctly, I do not know remember the name.
My aunt Betty, the one I was named after her, her husband committed suicide in our house.
So I mean, those were all things way before--
Well, he had been a soldier in the First World War.
And he was a very proud bearer of a German, German cross, I do not-- [NON-ENGLISH] cross.
That's it.
Iron Cross.
And he was a very proud-- and so when Hitler came, and they were-- you know, they-- they-- they lived in a big city.
And they got almost immediately the reaction of the Hitler and whatever he did, he came to Holland.
And he just didn't want to live anymore, so he committed suicide.
I never saw the actual fact, but I know that my father found him upstairs.
And so that's how I know.
Yes.
We had a-- my mother had a maid.
There was nothing special.
It was not that we were rich, not at all.
But we had a maid.
And my mother-- our maid would come each and every evening and would ask my mother, for how many people do you think I should cook today?
How many potatoes should I peel today?
Because there were at least, at least, each and every day, we were so close to the German border, that everybody would come across the border, right there where we were.
And if anybody saw them and they would ask a question, my father was the Jewish community.
So he would always try to get the people on a train to go to Amsterdam or wherever they were going to.
In fact, the youngest-- I think-- I was about 12, I think.

And since my father was so well-known also by the police and everything-- and we had a particular kind of police during that time-- he didn't always want to go to the border to pick up the people whom he knew were going to come.
How he always know, I don't know exactly anymore.
He would send me.
And I remember that I took a lady from Austria with a little boy who was, at the time, about four or five years old.
They stayed with us for quite some time at home.
I mean, maybe two months or so, something similar to that.
But this was-- that was normal, actually.
So I was--
Our house was a safe house.
Everybody got something to eat and then was put on a train or on a bus in order to go to the rest of Holland.
My father had the connection.
There was-- later on, there became something like Jewish-- I do not remember the-- [DUTCH], that's the Dutch word for it.
So there was a community.
They had all the connections, and they would catch-- or they would, let's say, gather all the Jews that came across the border.
Yes.
Much, much before.

My father, well, I think he knew what was happening, but-- and specially let's say because in 1940, when the war started, it started in the beginning of May of 1940, we were occupied within two minutes, within two minutes.
Because we were not even-- we were not even 10 minutes away from the border.
Oh, definitely.
Oh, definitely.
Well, there was very little to describe, because there were the German soldiers marching through your little town.
But actually, we had known already from the day before, because there were a lot of Dutch people who would work in Germany, right across the border.
And they came home and they would say, oh, tomorrow is going to invasion, because they told us so.
So we actually knew the day before.
But there was nothing that you could do about it.
OK?
By that time, 1940, my older brother, Jos-- because of his very, very good schooling and the things that he had had, he had been eligible for a position in the Dutch army.
And he was a second lieutenant.
I remember that he made a phone call to the house and warned us to be careful.
But there wasn't anything that he could do either.
But that's one of the things.
So I know that, that I remember.
Yes.
They marched into town and through town.
Through town.
Did not remain there.
There was one battalion that came, I think, maybe two weeks later, and stayed there.
But that was not the very day of the occupation.
The occupation started, and they had to fight their way into Germany-- or into Holland-- excuse me-- to, you know, get as far as possible.
So.
